skill.name: bash-defensive-patterns
skill.description: Master defensive Bash programming techniques for production-grade scripts. Use when writing robust shell scripts, CI/CD pipelines, or system utilities requiring fault tolerance and safety.
skill.summary: Build safer Bash scripts with strict mode, validation, cleanup, logging, and dry-run patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, filesystem, network, and weak cryptography patterns. Review found these are Markdown tutorial examples and resource links, not executable skill code or malicious behavior. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, credential theft, or hidden execution path was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Instructional Shell Command Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings occur inside fenced Bash examples that teach strict mode, command substitution, process handling, dependency checks, and dry-run wrappers. These examples are relevant to the skill purpose and are not executed by the skill itself, but generated scripts should still be reviewed before use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged shell patterns are visibly inside Markdown code fences and serve as defensive Bash examples. I found no separate executable file, installer, or runtime path that would run these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Operations Are Demonstration Patterns
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings cover safe temporary directory handling, cleanup traps, process waits using standard redirection, and cautious file move or removal examples. They are examples for defensive scripting and do not perform actions during skill loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear in explanatory Bash snippets and include defensive controls such as mktemp, traps, prompts, and quoting. The residual risk is that users may adapt these examples into real scripts without review.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Resources
low_findings.description: The network findings are three resource links listed under the Resources section. No evidence found that the skill fetches these URLs or sends data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plain Markdown references at the end of the guide. There is no surrounding code that performs network access or transmits user data.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography blocker alerts do not correspond to implemented cryptographic code. The reviewed locations are prose or generic Bash examples, and no hashing, encryption, or credential handling logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the reported areas found variable names, prose, and file operation examples rather than cryptographic APIs. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms being used.

content.value_statement: Bash scripts often fail in production because they do not handle errors, inputs, or cleanup safely. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create defensive shell scripts with clear safety patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains Bash strict mode and error trap patterns.
Shows safe variable quoting, arrays, and conditional checks.
Provides templates for argument parsing and validation.
Covers temporary file cleanup and atomic file writes.
Demonstrates structured logging and dry-run support.
Includes dependency checks for common command-line tools.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not execute or verify scripts.
Examples require adaptation for each operating system and shell version.
It focuses on Bash, not POSIX sh, PowerShell, or Python automation.
Generated destructive commands still need human review before running.

use_cases.title: Harden Deployment Scripts
use_cases.description: Convert fragile release scripts into safer Bash with strict mode, cleanup, validation, and dry-run support.

use_cases.title: Review CI Shell Steps
use_cases.description: Improve CI pipeline commands by adding predictable error handling, dependency checks, and clearer logging.

use_cases.title: Create Admin Utilities
use_cases.description: Draft system maintenance scripts that validate inputs and handle temporary files more safely.

prompt_templates.title: Add Strict Mode
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner hardening for a small script.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Bash script and add defensive basics: strict mode, quoted variables, clear errors, and safe cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Safe CLI
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a script with predictable arguments.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Bash utility with named options, usage output, required argument validation, structured logging, and dry-run mode.

prompt_templates.title: Harden File Processing
prompt_templates.scenario: Process files without unsafe splitting or overwrite risk.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this file processing script to use NUL-safe iteration, temporary files, atomic writes, and guarded cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Production Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before running automation in production.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this production Bash automation for error handling, idempotency, command injection risk, unsafe deletion, and rollback gaps.

output_examples.input: A deployment script exits silently when one command fails.
output_examples.output: The response identifies missing strict mode, adds an error trap, improves logging, and explains where cleanup should run.

output_examples.input: A CI job uses unquoted variables and basic file loops.
output_examples.output: The response recommends quoted variables, safer conditionals, NUL-safe file iteration, and dependency checks before the job starts.

output_examples.input: A maintenance script deletes temporary files and supports dry-run.
output_examples.output: The response suggests mktemp usage, scoped cleanup traps, dry-run command wrappers, and explicit safeguards for destructive actions.

best_practices: Review generated scripts before running them on real systems.
Use dry-run mode and test fixtures before production data.
Document required tools, permissions, and supported platforms.
anti_patterns: Do not copy destructive examples into production without safeguards.
Do not disable strict mode to hide script failures.
Do not pass untrusted input into shell commands without validation.

faq.question: Does this skill run shell commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides Bash guidance and examples for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it safe for production scripts?
faq.answer: It can improve production scripts, but every generated command should be reviewed and tested first.

faq.question: Does it support POSIX sh?
faq.answer: It focuses on Bash. Some guidance applies to POSIX sh, but Bash-specific syntax is common.

faq.question: Can it help with CI/CD pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes. It is useful for hardening shell steps in build, test, release, and deployment pipelines.

faq.question: Does it detect every security issue?
faq.answer: No. It is a pattern guide, not a complete security scanner or policy engine.

faq.question: What should I provide in a prompt?
faq.answer: Provide the script, target environment, allowed commands, failure requirements, and any destructive operations.
